content.text: ```json { "module": "ui-design-system", "scores": { "visual_polish": 62, "domain_depth": 58, "data_richness": 55, "interaction_quality": 60, "ai_integration": 65, "mobile_first": 52, "performance": 63, "typescript_quality": 70, "error_handling": 58, "realtime": 60, "accessibility": 48, "security": 72, "differentiation": 55, "enterprise_readiness": 60 }, "overall_score": 60, "issues": [ { "id": "ui-design-system-C1-001", "severity": "critical", "dimension": "accessibility", "title": "EmptyState component missing ARIA role and landmark attributes", "file": "src/components/ui/EmptyState.tsx", "description": "The EmptyState component renders a decorative container with no ARIA role, no aria-label, and no live region announcement. Screen readers cannot identify empty states as meaningful UI feedback, violating WCAG 2.1 AA. The action button also lacks a descriptive aria-label when icon-only.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/ui/EmptyState.tsx, make these specific changes to the EmptyState component function: BEFORE: The root container div has no ARIA role and no aria-label attribute. The optional icon element and the action button have no accessible labels. The component renders as a plain div with no semantic meaning for assistive technologies. AFTER: Add role=\"status\" and aria-label={`Empty state: ${title}`} to the root container div. Add aria-hidden=\"true\" to any decorative icon element. Ensure the primary action button always has an aria-label prop that concatenates the action text with context (e.g., aria-label={`${actionLabel} — ${title}`}). Wrap the title in an id and reference it with aria-labelledby on the container instead of aria-label when a title prop is present. Add a sr-only CSS class span as the first child that announces the empty state to screen readers: `{title} — no items to display`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C1-002", "severity": "critical", "dimension": "error_handling", "title": "Skeletons component has no timeout fallback — infinite pulse on fetch failure", "file": "src/components/ui/Skeletons.tsx", "description": "The skeleton loader components pulse indefinitely if the parent query fails or never resolves. There is no maximum display duration, no error boundary integration, and no fallback to an error state after a timeout threshold. Users with slow or failed connections see an infinite loading animation with no feedback.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/ui/Skeletons.tsx, make these specific changes to every exported skeleton wrapper component (e.g., TableSkeleton, CardSkeleton, MetricCardSkeleton, or whatever named exports exist): BEFORE: Each skeleton component renders a static pulsing gray rectangle with no internal timeout logic. It continues to pulse indefinitely regardless of how long the parent has been waiting, and it has no mechanism to communicate a timeout to parent components. AFTER: Add an optional `timeoutMs` prop (default: 15000) and an optional `onTimeout` callback prop typed as `() => void` to each skeleton component's props interface. Inside each component, add a useEffect that sets a setTimeout for `timeoutMs` milliseconds. When the timeout fires, call `onTimeout?.()` and set a local `timedOut` boolean state to true. When `timedOut` is true, render a fallback div with className containing the design-system danger color text (#E74C3C), an AlertTriangle icon from lucide-react at 20px, and the text \"Taking longer than expected. Please refresh.\" instead of the skeleton rectangles. Clear the timeout in the useEffect cleanup function. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C1-003", "severity": "critical", "dimension": "mobile_first", "title": "Primitives.tsx interactive elements below 44px touch target minimum", "file": "src/components/Primitives.tsx", "description": "Button, Badge, and IconButton primitives in Primitives.tsx use hardcoded height values (h-8, h-9, py-1.5) that produce touch targets below Apple's 44px minimum guideline. On mobile field use with gloves, these are effectively untappable, directly violating the product's field-first mandate.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/Primitives.tsx, make these specific changes to the Button, IconButton, and Badge component definitions: BEFORE: The Button component uses className values that result in heights of 32px (h-8) or 36px (h-9) for sm and md size variants. The IconButton uses p-1 or p-1.5 padding producing a tap target under 36px. The Badge component uses py-0.5 and px-2 producing a very small hit area. None of these components have a minimum touch target enforcement for mobile. AFTER: Update the Button size variants so that sm = min-h-[40px], md = min-h-[44px], lg = min-h-[52px] using Tailwind's min-h utility. Update IconButton to use a minimum of p-2.5 on all variants, ensuring the rendered element is at least 44x44px — add explicit w-11 h-11 (44px) as the minimum for all icon button sizes. For Badge, add min-h-[28px] and min-w-[28px] so interactive badges meet touch requirements. Add a `data-touch-target` attribute set to \"true\" on each of these components for automated testing hooks. Do not change the visual appearance for desktop (the min-h will be overridden naturally by content on desktop). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C1-004", "severity": "high", "dimension": "visual_polish", "title": "ShortcutOverlay has no keyboard trap and no focus management on open/close", "file": "src/components/ui/ShortcutOverlay.tsx", "description": "The ShortcutOverlay modal component does not trap focus inside the overlay when open, does not move focus to the modal on open, and does not restore focus to the triggering element on close. This breaks keyboard navigation and fails WCAG 2.1 criterion 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap) by allowing focus to escape behind the overlay.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/ui/ShortcutOverlay.tsx, make these specific changes to the ShortcutOverlay component function: BEFORE: The component renders an overlay div and its children without any focus management. When the overlay opens, focus remains on whatever element was focused before. When the overlay closes, focus is not restored. There is no focus trap mechanism — pressing Tab while the overlay is open moves focus behind the overlay into the page content beneath. AFTER: (1) Add a useRef(null) called `overlayRef` attached to the outermost focusable container div of the overlay. (2) Add a useEffect that runs when the `isOpen` (or equivalent open/visible) prop becomes true: store `document.activeElement as HTMLElement` in a `previousFocus` ref, then call `overlayRef.current?.focus()` after a 0ms setTimeout to allow the DOM to paint. (3) Add a cleanup/close useEffect that calls `previousFocus.current?.focus()` when the component unmounts or `isOpen` becomes false. (4) Add a keydown event listener inside the overlay's useEffect that intercepts Tab and Shift+Tab, queries all focusable elements within `overlayRef.current` using the selector `button, [href], input, select, textarea, [tabindex]:not([tabindex='-1'])`, and cycles focus within that list. (5) Add `tabIndex={-1}` to the overlay container div and `role=\"dialog\"` with `aria-modal=\"true\"` and `aria-label=\"Keyboard shortcuts\"`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C1-005", "severity": "high", "dimension": "interaction_quality", "title": "ConnectionStatus component shows raw technical error strings to end users", "file": "src/components/ui/ConnectionStatus.tsx", "description": "The ConnectionStatus component passes raw WebSocket or Supabase error messages directly into the visible UI text. Messages like 'WebSocket connection to wss://... failed' or 'Error: JWT expired' appear verbatim to superintendents on job sites. These are untranslated, technical, and unhelpful to the target user.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/ui/ConnectionStatus.tsx, make these specific changes to the ConnectionStatus component function: BEFORE: The component renders `error.message` or a similar raw error string property directly inside a visible span, p, or div element that is shown to the user when the connection fails or is in a degraded state. The raw string from the underlying WebSocket or Supabase client is passed through without transformation. AFTER: Create a pure function inside the file called `getConnectionErrorMessage(rawError: string | Error | null | undefined): string` that maps known error patterns to user-friendly strings: if rawError contains 'JWT' or 'token' or '401' return 'Session expired — please refresh the page'; if rawError contains 'WebSocket' or 'network' or 'ERR_NETWORK' return 'Connection lost — working offline, changes will sync when reconnected'; if rawError contains '503' or 'unavailable' return 'Server temporarily unavailable — retrying…'; for all other cases return 'Connection issue — tap to retry'. Replace every instance of `error.message` or raw error string interpolation in the JSX with a call to `getConnectionErrorMessage(error)`. Add an `aria-live=\"polite\"` attribute to the container element that shows the error message so screen readers announce connection status changes. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." } ], "invented_features": [ { "id": "inv-001", "title": "Construction-Aware Design Token Theming Engine", "description": "A CSS custom property system in tokens.css where tokens automatically shift between 'field mode' (high contrast, large text, maximum touch targets for outdoor sunlight and gloves) and 'office mode' (standard density, richer data display) based on a user preference toggle stored in localStorage. Zero new backend required — pure CSS variable switching via a data-mode attribute on the root HTML element." }, { "id": "inv-002", "title": "Metric Card Animated Delta Indicator", "description": "Extend the existing metric card pattern so that when a numeric value changes (via real-time subscription or query refetch), the value animates from old to new using useAnimatedNumber (already exists at src/hooks/useAnimatedNumber.ts) and a colored flash (green for improvement, red for regression) pulses briefly using the RealtimeFlash component (already at src/components/ui/RealtimeFlash.tsx). No backend needed — purely wires existing hooks and components together." } ], "competitive_intel": "Procore's UI is functional but dense and dated — its mobile app is an adapted desktop experience with small touch targets and complex navigation. Fieldwire leads on mobile UX with true swipe gestures and offline-first design but lacks financial depth. SiteSyncAI's design system has the right foundations (token system, skeleton loaders, ARIA components) but is not yet enforcing its own standards consistently: touch targets fall below its stated 44px minimum, keyboard focus management is incomplete in modal overlays, and error states surface raw technical strings that would confuse a superintendent in the field. Closing these gaps would put SiteSync's mobile UX clearly ahead of Procore and competitive with Fieldwire while maintaining the financial depth neither competitor has.", "summary": "The UI Design System module shows a solid architectural foundation — a token-based theme file, skeleton loader components, ARIA utilities, and a connection status indicator are all present and structured correctly. The TypeScript quality is reasonable with typed props and consistent patterns. However, several execution gaps prevent this from meeting the 'would Apple ship this' standard the product vision demands. The most critical issues are: accessibility failures in the EmptyState and ShortcutOverlay components that would fail a WCAG audit; skeleton loaders that provide no timeout fallback leaving users staring at infinite pulses on network failure; interactive primitives with touch targets below the 44px minimum that would frustrate a superintendent wearing gloves; and raw technical error strings surfacing in the ConnectionStatus component that would confuse field users. The mobile-first score (52) is the weakest dimension because the stated design standard of 44px touch targets is not consistently enforced in the Primitives layer. Fixing these five issues would bring the module from 60 to approximately 74 overall and close the most significant gap between the current implementation and the world-class standard the product vision requires." } ```
